%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 5.2-5 Convolution Property

> **Section Objective:** Learn how convolution, LTID system response, and initial/final values are handled directly in the z-domain.

## Concepts In This Section

- Time-convolution property
- LTID zero-state response
- Initial value theorem
- Final value theorem

## 1. Time-convolution property

This is **Eq. (5.19)**. When two sequences \(x_1[n]\) and \(x_2[n]\) are convolved in the time domain — written with the \(*\) symbol — their z-transforms simply multiply together as \(X_1[z] \cdot X_2[z]\).

**When to use it:** Any time an exam problem shows \(x_1[n] * x_2[n]\), or writes an LTID response as a convolution sum, apply this property immediately.

**Minimal example:** If \(x_1[n] \Longleftrightarrow X_1[z]\) and \(x_2[n] \Longleftrightarrow X_2[z]\), do not compute the convolution sum first — take the z-transform of each sequence and multiply the results.

#### Common Misuse

Time-domain **pointwise multiplication** \(x_1[n]\,x_2[n]\) is **not** covered by this property. The property is triggered only by the convolution symbol \(*\), not by two sequences placed side by side.

$$x_1[n]*x_2[n]\Longleftrightarrow X_1[z]X_2[z]$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="web_reference_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Convolution](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/21/Comparison_convolution_correlation.svg)
*Reference visual from Wikipedia: Convolution*
%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-reference-source"><a href="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/21/Comparison_convolution_correlation.svg" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Reference image from Wikipedia</a></div>%%KC_END%%

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. LTID zero-state response

This is **Eq. (5.20)**. An LTID system with zero-state response satisfies \(y[n] = x[n] * h[n]\) in the time domain. Applying Eq. (5.19) immediately gives \(Y[z] = X[z]H[z]\).

- \(X[z]\): z-transform of the input signal
- \(H[z]\): z-transform of the impulse response (the system's transfer function)
- \(Y[z]\): z-transform of the output

**When to use it:** When the problem gives an input and a transfer function or impulse response, and asks for the output.

**Exam trigger:** Words like *LTID system*, *impulse response*, *zero-state response*, or *find the output* all point to this formula.

#### Common Misuse

Multiply by \(H[z]\) only when computing the **zero-state** response. If the problem includes separate initial-condition terms, those must be handled independently — do not fold them into \(X[z]H[z]\).

$$Y[z]=X[z]H[z]$$

## 3. Initial value theorem

For a **causal** sequence, the very first sample \(x[0]\) can be read directly from \(X[z]\) by letting \(z \to \infty\) — no inverse z-transform needed.

- \(x[0]\): the first sample of the causal sequence
- \(X[z]\): its z-transform

**When to use it:** When an exam asks for the initial value without requiring the full inverse transform.

**How to apply it:** If \(X[z]\) is a rational expression, compare the degrees of the numerator and denominator. If the degree of the numerator is less than the denominator, the limit is 0. If degrees are equal, the limit is the ratio of leading coefficients. Alternatively, rewrite in powers of \(z^{-1}\) and let \(z \to \infty\) so all \(z^{-1}\) terms vanish.

#### Common Misuse

Do not apply this theorem to a non-causal sequence. Also, do not confuse the direction: the initial value uses \(z \to \infty\), not \(z \to 0\).

$$x[0]=\lim_{z\to\infty}X[z]$$

## 4. Final value theorem

This theorem gives the **steady-state** (long-run) value of a sequence directly from the z-domain, without computing the full inverse transform.

- \(\lim_{N\to\infty}x[N]\): the value the sequence settles to as \(N\) grows large
- \((z-1)X[z]\): the modified transform whose behavior at \(z=1\) gives the answer

**Required condition:** Before applying the formula, verify that \((z-1)X[z]\) has **no poles outside the unit circle**. If any pole of \((z-1)X[z]\) lies outside \(|z|=1\), the sequence does not converge to a finite final value and the theorem must not be used.

**When to use it:** Questions asking for *final value*, *steady-state value*, or *long-run behavior*.

**How to apply it:** Factor or inspect \((z-1)X[z]\), locate all poles, confirm they are inside or on the unit circle (with the \(z=1\) pole already cancelled by the \((z-1)\) factor), then evaluate the limit.

#### Common Misuse

Plugging \(z=1\) into the formula before checking poles is the most common exam error. If the sequence oscillates or diverges, the theorem gives a meaningless number.

$$\lim_{N\to\infty}x[N]=\lim_{z\to1}(z-1)X[z]$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Eq. (5.19): Time-domain convolution becomes z-domain multiplication — \(x_1[n]*x_2[n]\Longleftrightarrow X_1[z]X_2[z]\). Triggered by the \(*\) symbol, not pointwise multiplication.
- Eq. (5.20): LTID zero-state response — \(Y[z]=X[z]H[z]\). Use when the problem gives input and impulse response; applies to zero-state terms only.
- Initial value theorem (causal sequences only): \(x[0]=\lim_{z\to\infty}X[z]\). Use \(z\to\infty\), not \(z\to 0\).
- Final value theorem: \(\lim_{N\to\infty}x[N]=\lim_{z\to1}(z-1)X[z]\) — valid only when \((z-1)X[z]\) has no poles outside the unit circle; always check the pole condition first.

*Next, use these properties as a shortcut toolbox for solving z-transform system problems.*
